title: Month-End Close
subhead: Every account you've reconciled, every invoice you've entered, every payroll run you've booked — month-end close is where it all gets pulled together, checked, and signed off. This is the module where you stop doing individual accounting tasks and start running an actual close.
hook: "Walk me through your month-end close process." Every accounting interview asks some version of this question. Most candidates can name a step or two. You're about to be able to walk through all 15 — with a real close you actually ran.

chunks.title: Why closing the books takes 3-5 days, not 3-5 minutes
chunks.bodyHtml:
QuickBooks can print a profit and loss statement in two seconds. So why do real companies take 3 to 5 business days after month-end to actually close the books? Because printing a report and trusting a report are two different things.

Month-end close is the process of reviewing, reconciling, adjusting, and finalizing the books for a completed month, so that when the CEO or a lender looks at the numbers, those numbers are actually true:

  • Review — check that sales, bills, payments, payroll, and bank activity are all fully entered.
  • Reconcile — compare the books to outside proof: bank statements, AR/AP aging, payroll reports, loan statements, fixed asset schedules.
  • Adjust — post timing entries: accruals, prepaids, depreciation, payroll liabilities, interest.
  • Finalize — prepare reports, save workpapers, get manager approval, and lock the period so prior numbers can't silently change.

Skip any one of those four steps and the P&L you hand the CEO is a guess wearing a suit. Skarion Manufacturing targets a 5-business-day close, because accuracy is worth more than speed — a fast close that's wrong just means restating everything next month.

callout.html: Interviewers ask about your close process because it reveals whether you understand accounting as a system of proof, not just data entry. "I close the books in a day" is not usually the answer they're hoping for.

chunks.title: The 15 standard close steps
chunks.bodyHtml:
Skarion's close follows the same 15 steps used across small and mid-size companies. Learn this list — it's the backbone of every close interview question you'll get.

#StepWhat it means
1Confirm cutoffTransactions land in the correct month — January expenses stay in January even if paid in February.
2Collect source documentsBank statements, credit card statements, invoices, payroll reports, loan statements, inventory records.
3Record sales and ARAll customer invoices, sales receipts, credit memos, and payments — review unapplied cash.
4Record vendor bills and APAll vendor bills, expense reports, recurring expenses, and vendor credits.
5Record cash activityDeposits, checks, ACH, wires, bank fees, interest income, transfers, loan payments.
6Reconcile bank accountsMatch bank statement activity to the GL — find outstanding checks, deposits in transit, duplicates.
7Reconcile credit cardsMatch card charges and payments to the GL, confirm receipts are attached.
8Reconcile AR aging to GLThe AR aging total must equal the AR balance on the balance sheet.
9Reconcile AP aging to GLThe AP aging total must equal the AP balance on the balance sheet.
10Review inventory and COGSCheck purchases, usage, ending inventory, and cost of goods sold against the physical count.
11Record payroll and liabilitiesGross wages, withholdings, employer taxes, benefit deductions, net pay.
12Update fixed assets and depreciationNew assets, disposals, monthly depreciation, reconcile the fixed asset schedule to the GL.
13Record accruals and prepaidsExpenses incurred but not billed yet, and amortized prepaid items.
14Review financials and variancesTrial balance, P&L, balance sheet, cash flow — explain unusual changes.
15Finalize, approve, and lockSave workpapers, prepare the management report, get approval, lock the period.

Mapped to a calendar: Day 1 is transaction entry, Day 2 is reconciliation, Day 3 is adjusting entries, Day 4 is review, Day 5 is finalize and lock.

cards.back: Recording an expense or revenue in the period it was incurred or earned, even though cash hasn't moved yet. Example: a utility bill for services used in January but not received until February gets accrued in January.
Spreading the cost of something paid in advance (annual insurance, annual software) across the months it covers, instead of expensing it all at once.
Cash received before the work is done or the product is delivered — it sits as a liability until it's actually earned, then it moves to revenue.

A soft close is fast and uses estimates for small items — good for internal monthly reporting. A hard close fully reconciles every major account, posts every adjustment, and locks the period — required for quarter-end, year-end, audits, and loan reporting.
A listing of every GL account and its balance, used to confirm total debits equal total credits before financial statements are produced.
Journal entries posted at period-end to correct timing — depreciation, accruals, prepaids, payroll liabilities, interest — so the books reflect economic reality, not just cash movement.
Once a close is approved, the accounting system prevents further changes to that month's transactions without manager override — protects prior-period numbers from silent edits.

chunks.title: How January actually closed at Skarion
chunks.bodyHtml:
Here's what really happened during Skarion's January close, using the actual bank reconciliation.

The book balance per QuickBooks was $226,594.19. But that number was wrong — and Day 2 of the close (reconcile cash) is exactly where the problem surfaced. Working the bank rec turned up four separate issues:

AdjustmentAmount
Book balance per QuickBooks$226,594.19
Less: duplicate Coastal Electric deposit−$9,503.60
Add: Valley Freight payment correction ($2,410 entered, $2,140 cleared)+$270.00
Less: bank service fee missing from QuickBooks−$45.00
Add: bank interest missing from QuickBooks+$18.50
Adjusted book balance$217,334.09

The bank side, worked independently from the statement, landed on the exact same number: $211,161.25 statement balance, plus a $6,612.84 deposit in transit (Manassas Telecom), minus a $440.00 outstanding ADP payment, equals $217,334.09. Both sides met in the middle — that's how you know the reconciliation is right.

The biggest item was the duplicate deposit: a Coastal Electric payment had been entered into QuickBooks twice, which incorrectly cleared that customer's invoice a second time. Revenue was never overstated — the receivable was just erased by mistake. The fix reinstated it: Debit 1100 Accounts Receivable $9,503.60 / Credit 1000 Operating Checking $9,503.60.

callout.html: Notice what this proves: the close isn't paperwork for its own sake. Without Day 2's bank reconciliation, Skarion's books would have overstated cash by $9,503.60 — an error large enough to matter to a lender or the CEO — and nobody would have caught it until the money didn't show up somewhere later.

questions.q: Skarion's book balance per QuickBooks was $226,594.19, but the adjusted book balance came out to $217,334.09. What was the single largest adjustment causing that gap?
options: The duplicate Coastal Electric deposit of $9,503.60
The missing bank service fee of $45.00
questions.explanation: The duplicate deposit was by far the largest item, at $9,503.60 — it dwarfed the fee, interest, and freight corrections combined.

questions.q: The bank statement showed $211,161.25. After adding the deposit in transit and subtracting the outstanding ACH payment, what did the adjusted bank balance equal?
questions.explanation: $211,161.25 + $6,612.84 (deposit in transit) − $440.00 (outstanding ADP payment) = $217,334.09 — matching the adjusted book balance exactly.

questions.q: Which day of Skarion's 5-day close calendar is when the duplicate deposit would normally be caught?
questions.explanation: Bank reconciliation happens on Day 2. That's exactly when comparing the GL to the bank statement surfaces timing differences and errors like duplicate deposits.

questions.q: Why doesn't the duplicate Coastal Electric deposit mean revenue was overstated?
options: Because duplicate deposits never affect revenue, only cash
Because the duplicate entry cleared the same invoice twice, which erased the receivable rather than creating new revenue
Because Coastal Electric's invoice was never actually recorded
Because sales tax absorbed the difference
questions.explanation: The original invoice already recorded the revenue correctly. The duplicate deposit just applied a second cash receipt against the same invoice, wiping out the receivable balance incorrectly — the fix reinstates AR, it doesn't touch revenue.

chunks.title: Post January's depreciation entry
chunks.scenarioHtml: Step 12 of the close is updating fixed assets and depreciation. Skarion's fixed asset schedule covers 10 assets — CNC brake press, laser cutter, forklift, delivery van, air compressor system, office furniture, computers, QA testing station, warehouse pallet racking, and a label printer/packaging line. Combined, January's straight-line monthly depreciation across all 10 assets totals $5,208.10. Post the adjusting entry.

chunks.title: Run Skarion's month-end close checklist
chunks.instructionsHtml:
Download the Month-End Close Playbook below. Using the 18-row sample checklist (C-01 through C-18) as your template, complete a close checklist for Skarion's January close:

  1. For each of the 18 tasks, mark a status (Not Started, In Progress, Waiting, Complete, or Reviewed) as if you were the staff accountant running this close.
  2. For the bank reconciliation task (C-06), note the real result: adjusted book and bank balances both equal $217,334.09, driven mainly by the $9,503.60 duplicate Coastal Electric deposit.
  3. For the fixed assets task (C-12), note the depreciation entry: $5,208.10 across 10 assets.
  4. Identify which tasks would move faster under a soft close versus which absolutely require a hard close.

Then write a one-page January Financial Summary memo for the CEO — the same deliverable Skarion's staff accountant would submit on Day 5. Cover: net sales, gross profit and margin, ending AR and AP, the bank reconciliation result, and any issues you'd flag for follow-up.
chunks.reflectionPrompt: Write your January Financial Summary memo for the CEO: what were the headline numbers, what went wrong during the close, and what would you fix before February?
